objective.content_objective: Students will identify the three states of matter and their properties
objective.student_goal: I will describe solids, liquids, and gases using scientific vocabulary
objective.wida_objective: Students will use descriptive language to compare and contrast states of matter (ELD Standard 4, Levels 2-4)

anticipatory_set.original_content: Show ice cube melting in a beaker. Ask: What is happening? Why? Record observations.
anticipatory_set.bilingual_bridge: Preview key cognates: solid/sólido, liquid/líquido, gas/gás, matter/matéria, molecule/molécula. Display visual chart with images and labels in both languages.

tailored_instruction.original_content: Present three states of matter with demonstrations. Show solid (ice), liquid (water), gas (steam). Discuss molecular movement in each state. Students complete comparison chart.

co_teaching_model.implementation: Three stations: (1) Hands-on exploration with materials, (2) Digital simulation of molecular movement, (3) Vocabulary building with bilingual flashcards
co_teaching_model.teacher_roles: Teacher A manages stations 1-2, Teacher B leads station 3 with ELL focus, students rotate every 12 minutes

ell_support.implementation: Use particle diagrams showing molecular arrangement in solids, liquids, and gases. Display anchor chart with images and bilingual labels.
Provide frames: 'A solid has ___ shape and ___ volume.' 'Molecules in a liquid are ___ than in a solid.' Post frames in English and Portuguese.
Highlight Portuguese-English cognates throughout lesson. Create cognate word wall for science vocabulary.

misconceptions.original_content: Students may think gases have no mass or that liquids always take the shape of their container from the bottom up.

linguistic_note.common_confusion: Portuguese speakers may confuse 'matter' with 'material' (matéria vs. material). Both exist in Portuguese but with different meanings.
linguistic_note.clarification: Emphasize that 'matter' (matéria) refers to anything that has mass and takes up space, while 'material' (material) refers to the substance something is made from.

assessment.primary_assessment: Students complete three-column chart comparing states of matter. Must include at least 3 properties for each state.

bilingual_overlay.accommodation: ELL students (Levels 1-2) may use drawings with labels. Levels 3-4 use sentence frames. Levels 5-6 write full sentences.
bilingual_overlay.language_support: Provide bilingual word bank and visual references during assessment.

homework.original_content: Find 5 examples of each state of matter at home. Draw or photograph them. Label in English.
homework.family_connection: Ask family members to help identify examples. Discuss: What do you call these states of matter in Portuguese? (sólido, líquido, gasoso)

objective.content_objective: Students will explain how matter changes from one state to another
objective.student_goal: I will describe melting, freezing, evaporation, and condensation
objective.wida_objective: Students will use cause-and-effect language to explain phase changes (ELD Standard 4, Levels 3-5)

anticipatory_set.original_content: Review homework examples. Discuss: Can matter change from one state to another? How?
anticipatory_set.bilingual_bridge: Introduce phase change vocabulary with cognates: evaporation/evaporação, condensation/condensação. Show video of water cycle with bilingual captions.

tailored_instruction.original_content: Demonstrate phase changes: melt ice, boil water, observe condensation on cold glass. Introduce terms: melting, freezing, evaporation, condensation. Students conduct mini-experiments in groups.

co_teaching_model.implementation: Divide class by language proficiency. Both groups do same experiments but with differentiated language support.
co_teaching_model.teacher_roles: Teacher A works with higher proficiency students using academic language. Teacher B works with emerging bilinguals using simplified language, visuals, and L1 support.

ell_support.implementation: Use cycle diagram to show phase changes. Include arrows with temperature indicators and bilingual labels.
All students conduct experiments to observe phase changes directly. Reduces language barrier through concrete experience.

misconceptions.original_content: Students may think evaporation only happens when water boils. Some may not understand that freezing and melting are reverse processes.

linguistic_note.common_confusion: The word 'evaporation' may be confused with 'vaporization' (both translate to evaporação/vaporização in Portuguese).
linguistic_note.clarification: Explain that evaporation happens at any temperature, while boiling (ebulição) happens at a specific temperature.

assessment.primary_assessment: Lab report: Describe observations from experiments. Explain what caused each phase change.

bilingual_overlay.accommodation: Provide sentence starters: 'When we heated the ice, it ___ because ___.' Allow use of diagrams with labels.
bilingual_overlay.language_support: Word bank with phase change terms in English and Portuguese. Visual reference chart available.

homework.original_content: Observe and record one phase change at home (ice melting, water boiling, steam condensing, etc.). Write 3-5 sentences describing what happened.
homework.family_connection: Discuss with family: What phase changes do you observe when cooking? Share examples in any language.

objective.content_objective: Students will investigate the role of temperature in phase changes
objective.student_goal: I will explain how adding or removing heat causes matter to change state
objective.wida_objective: Students will use scientific language to describe cause-and-effect relationships (ELD Standard 4, Levels 3-5)

anticipatory_set.original_content: Quick write: What happens to ice cream on a hot day? Why? Share with partner.
anticipatory_set.bilingual_bridge: Review temperature vocabulary: hot/quente, cold/frio, heat/calor, temperature/temperatura. Use thermometer visuals with dual labels.

tailored_instruction.original_content: Investigate relationship between temperature and phase changes. Students measure temperature while ice melts and water boils. Graph results. Discuss energy transfer.

ell_support.implementation: Students create line graphs showing temperature over time. Visual representation reduces language demands while reinforcing concepts.
Mixed-proficiency groups. Each student has a role: timekeeper, temperature reader, recorder, reporter. Roles rotate.

misconceptions.original_content: Students may think temperature continues to rise during melting or boiling. They may not understand that energy is used to change state, not increase temperature.

linguistic_note.common_confusion: Heat (calor) vs. temperature (temperatura) - these are often used interchangeably in everyday Portuguese but have specific meanings in science.
linguistic_note.clarification: Heat is energy transfer; temperature is a measure of average kinetic energy. Use concrete examples and visuals to distinguish.

assessment.primary_assessment: Graph analysis: Students interpret their temperature graphs and answer questions about phase changes and energy.

bilingual_overlay.accommodation: Questions provided in simplified English with key terms in Portuguese. Sentence frames for written responses.
bilingual_overlay.language_support: Allow students to annotate graphs in Portuguese, then translate key points to English with support.

homework.original_content: Complete graph analysis worksheet. Write a paragraph explaining what happens to temperature during phase changes.
homework.family_connection: Ask family: How do you know when water is boiling? What do you observe? Discuss in any language.

objective.content_objective: Students will compare the water cycle to phase changes studied in class
objective.student_goal: I will identify phase changes in the water cycle and explain their causes
objective.wida_objective: Students will connect scientific concepts using academic vocabulary and complex sentences (ELD Standard 4, Levels 4-6)

anticipatory_set.original_content: Show water cycle diagram. Ask: Where do you see phase changes happening? Label them.
anticipatory_set.bilingual_bridge: Introduce water cycle terms: precipitation/precipitação, runoff/escoamento, collection/acumulação. Use animated diagram with bilingual narration.

tailored_instruction.original_content: Connect lab observations to real-world water cycle. Students create annotated water cycle diagrams showing phase changes and temperature conditions. Present to class.

co_teaching_model.implementation: Both teachers co-present, alternating between English and Portuguese for key concepts. Model translanguaging as a learning strategy.
co_teaching_model.teacher_roles: Teacher A presents in English, Teacher B clarifies in Portuguese and provides examples. Both answer questions and facilitate discussion.

ell_support.implementation: Allow students to discuss concepts in Portuguese, then present in English. Validate use of L1 as a cognitive tool.
Students create diagrams with labels, arrows, and brief explanations. Reduces language demands while demonstrating understanding.

misconceptions.original_content: Students may think the water cycle only happens in nature, not in everyday life. Some may not recognize that the same water is recycled.

linguistic_note.common_confusion: Cycle (ciclo) may be confused with circle (círculo). Both are similar in Portuguese.
linguistic_note.clarification: Emphasize that a cycle is a repeating process, not just a circular shape. Use timeline and circular diagram to show difference.

assessment.primary_assessment: Water cycle diagram with annotations. Must show at least 4 phase changes and explain temperature conditions for each.

bilingual_overlay.accommodation: Rubric includes options for different proficiency levels. Levels 1-2 use labels and simple phrases. Levels 3-4 use sentences. Levels 5-6 use complex sentences with transitions.
bilingual_overlay.language_support: Provide sentence frames for explanations: 'When ___ happens, water changes from ___ to ___ because ___.' Word bank available.

homework.original_content: Observe the water cycle at home or in your neighborhood. Take photos or draw pictures. Write where you see each part of the cycle.
homework.family_connection: Discuss with family: How does the water cycle affect our daily lives? Where does our water come from? Share in any language.

objective.content_objective: Students will demonstrate understanding of states of matter and phase changes
objective.student_goal: I will show what I learned about matter and how it changes
objective.wida_objective: Students will use scientific language to explain concepts in multiple formats (ELD Standard 4, Levels 2-6)

anticipatory_set.original_content: Review game: States of Matter Jeopardy. Teams compete to answer questions. Review key concepts.
anticipatory_set.bilingual_bridge: Game includes questions in both English and Portuguese. Students can answer in either language, then translate.

tailored_instruction.original_content: Brief review of key concepts. Students complete unit assessment with multiple formats: multiple choice, diagram labeling, short answer, and practical demonstration.

co_teaching_model.implementation: Most students take standard assessment. Small group of emerging bilinguals receives modified assessment with additional supports.
co_teaching_model.teacher_roles: Teacher A administers standard assessment to main group. Teacher B works with small group, providing scaffolds, clarifications, and extended time.

ell_support.implementation: Multiple ways to show understanding: drawing, labeling, writing, demonstrating. Students choose format that best shows their knowledge.
ELL students receive 1.5x time. Can use bilingual dictionary and reference materials.

misconceptions.original_content: Address any remaining misconceptions through one-on-one or small group support during assessment.

linguistic_note.common_confusion: Review all key vocabulary one final time, emphasizing cognates and false cognates.
linguistic_note.clarification: Provide visual glossary as reference during assessment.

assessment.primary_assessment: Unit test covering states of matter, phase changes, temperature, and water cycle. Multiple formats to accommodate different proficiency levels.

bilingual_overlay.accommodation: Test available in three versions: standard, modified with scaffolds, and bilingual with Portuguese supports. Students take version appropriate for their proficiency level.
bilingual_overlay.language_support: All versions include visual supports, word banks, and sentence frames. Bilingual dictionaries allowed.

homework.original_content: Reflection: Write about one thing you learned this week and one question you still have. Optional: Create a poster showing states of matter for extra credit.
homework.family_connection: Share your assessment results with family. Teach them one thing you learned about matter. Discuss in any language.
